current_fact.fact_text: Also on March 19, 1945, Adolf Hitler issued the so‑called Nero Decree, ordering the destruction of Germany’s own infrastructure, industries, and transport as Allied forces advanced. It was an apocalyptic command to leave nothing useful behind, invoking the burning of ancient Rome and revealing a regime ready to sacrifice its future along with itself.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: Faced with a leader intent on total self-destruction, the idea of a 'destination' elsewhere—another planet, another way of living—gains weight; this exploratory, boundary-crossing episode offers an imaginative escape route.

queue.fact_text: The Battle of Yamen’s end to the Song dynasty in 1279 (also commemorated on March 19) closed a period famed for advances in printing, gunpowder, and navigation. Even as the last court drowned at sea, the dynasty’s innovations—like movable type and paper money—were already reshaping life far beyond China’s borders.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The end of a brilliant but doomed era mirrors the launch of a new creative 'dynasty' for Sedna FM; from collapse in one corner of history to a charged, foundational beginning in another.

queue.fact_text: The creation of time zones in 1918 didn’t just standardize clocks; it quietly rewired daily life, from train timetables to sleep patterns. For the first time, entire regions agreed to move in sync, trading some local solar time for the convenience of living by shared, abstract hours.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A world newly ruled by standardized time pairs with an episode that gently resists clock pressure, inviting listeners to step into a slower, more personal rhythm on a 'slow planet.'

queue.fact_text: The collapse of the Emley Moor mast in 1969 left millions briefly without their familiar TV channels, jolting people into awareness of the invisible infrastructure behind everyday entertainment. Replacement structures were designed with new safety measures, showing how one failure can quietly redesign the skyline.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A toppled broadcast tower and an invisible vocal effect both reveal how much we depend on hidden technologies—and how their failure or overuse can force us to rethink what we hear.

queue.fact_text: GRB 080319B’s brief naked‑eye visibility in 2008 meant that, for moments, a human standing in the dark could unknowingly see light that had traveled for billions of years. It was a cosmic reminder that even an ordinary night sky sometimes hides extraordinary, one‑time events flickering at the edge of perception.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A star’s death lighting up the universe for an instant sits perfectly with a 'Cosmic' themed mix: bright, futuristic, and driven by pulses that feel a bit like distant, rhythmic signals from space.

published.fact_text: In 1824, American sealer and explorer Benjamin Morrell departed Antarctica after a controversial voyage that he later described in a wildly embellished memoir. His claims of discovering new lands and fantastic resources were so exaggerated that many contemporaries accused him of fraud, turning his Antarctic journey into one of exploration’s strangest cautionary tales.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An Antarctic voyage and the mystery of the Southern Ocean fit a water-themed, slow, immersive set that feels like drifting through strange, remote seas.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1965, teenage diver E. Lee Spence located the wreck of the Confederate steamer SS Georgiana exactly 102 years after it sank. The ship, once touted as the most powerful Confederate cruiser and valued at over $50 million in modern terms, helped establish Spence as a pioneer of underwater archaeology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A shipwreck discovery and a sea-focused episode both live on the water; the sailing stories echo the romance and danger of 19th-century steamers under the waves.

published.fact_text: In 1979, the U.S. House of Representatives began broadcasting its daily proceedings on the cable network C‑SPAN. For the first time, anyone with a TV could quietly eavesdrop on live democracy, watching debates unfold unedited instead of just hearing about them later in the news.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: C‑SPAN’s launch marks a new media-technology relationship with politics, mirroring the episode’s exploration of how screens and connectivity reshape how we experience public life.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1687, the French explorer René-Robert Cavelier de La Salle was murdered by his own men while searching for the mouth of the Mississippi River. His ambitious dream of a French North American empire collapsed in the Texas wilderness, a reminder of how fragile great expeditions could be when trust eroded.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: La Salle’s doomed trek across unfamiliar landscapes pairs with a reflective, landscape-inspired episode about journeys, mountains, and the stories we carry through difficult terrain.

published.fact_text: In 2011, after Libyan government forces failed to retake Benghazi, French jets launched Opération Harmattan, opening a multinational intervention in the Libyan Civil War. It was one of the first conflicts where live footage, social media, and global diplomacy intertwined in real time over a single city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A modern war sparked by uprising and foreign power resonates with an episode centered on protest music, resistance, and political tension.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1982, Argentine forces landed on the remote, icy island of South Georgia, escalating a confrontation with the United Kingdom that became the Falklands War. The clash began not on a main battlefield, but on a distant island dotted with glaciers, seals, and rusting whaling stations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: The stark, polar island setting of South Georgia aligns with an Arctic ocean episode, contrasting human conflict against the quiet presence of cold seas and marine life.

published.fact_text: In 1279, the Mongol victory at the naval Battle of Yamen ended the Song dynasty in China. The last Song ruler reputedly clutched a child emperor as they leapt into the sea, marking a dramatic close to centuries of Chinese innovation, poetry, and artistic flourishing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: The fall of a dynasty and a decisive sea battle pair with a cinematic, boundary-blurring trip-hop episode whose mood feels like the end of an era and the start of something entirely new.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1945, a Japanese dive bomber struck the American aircraft carrier USS Franklin off Japan, igniting massive fires that killed 724 crew members. Against all odds, the crippled ship refused to sink and limped back to the United States under her own power, becoming a symbol of grim endurance at sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A carrier engulfed in fire and explosions connects metaphorically to a high-voltage rock set, echoing the shock, danger, and raw energy of the attack and survival.

published.fact_text: In 1944, German forces occupied Hungary in a swift operation that reshaped the final year of World War II in Eastern Europe. The occupation accelerated the persecution of Hungarian Jews and turned the country into a late battleground between the collapsing Nazi regime and the advancing Red Army.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: The contrast between the cold machinery of occupation and the eruptive violence it unleashed echoes the episode’s imagery of fog and fire, with soulful songwriting as a counterpoint to war’s chaos.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1205, Latin Crusader forces under Henry of Flanders defeated the Nicaean army in the Battle of Adramyttion. It was an early test for the fragile Latin Empire that had been carved from the ruins of Byzantium just a year earlier, revealing how unstable and contested the new order truly was.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A medieval power struggle over empires meets an episode about claiming power and voice; the battle for territory becomes mirrored by women seizing space in a traditionally male rock world.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 2008, astronomers detected GRB 080319B, a colossal gamma-ray burst in the constellation Boötes that briefly became the most distant object ever visible to the naked eye. For less than a minute, someone looking at the right patch of night sky could have witnessed the death of a star halfway across the universe without a telescope.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic flash lighting up deep space fits naturally with an episode about reimagining emptiness and space, both literally and metaphorically.

published.fact_text: In 1969, the 385‑metre Emley Moor television mast in the UK collapsed dramatically after ice built up on its structure and high winds set it swaying. The fall temporarily silenced TV transmissions for millions, showing how fragile our towering symbols of broadcast technology can be against weather’s quiet force.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: The sudden collapse of a broadcasting tower and the birth of a new radio format both mark turning points in how signals travel through the air, from silence to new kinds of transmission.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1918, the U.S. Congress passed the Standard Time Act, formally establishing time zones and approving daylight saving time. Overnight, the country’s clocks became part of a coordinated system tied to railways, industry, and energy saving, turning time itself into a managed national infrastructure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A law that reorganizes how we measure hours connects neatly to a reflective episode about time, repetition, learning, and the slow, structured process of change.

published.fact_text: In 2023, amid a rapidly spreading banking crisis, the Swiss Federal Council brokered an emergency deal for UBS to acquire its rival Credit Suisse. The forced marriage of two financial giants in a single tense weekend was meant to calm global markets and prevent another cascade of failures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A financial panic and rushed merger benefit from a counterbalancing soundtrack about slowing down, shifting perspective, and finding calm amid transitions.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 2004, Taiwan’s President Chen Shui-bian was shot in an assassination attempt just one day before a closely contested presidential election. Wounded but surviving, he appeared bandaged before voters, and the incident became a flashpoint in Taiwan’s already polarized democracy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: An unexpected political injury on the eve of a critical decision pairs with an episode about bodily vulnerability, recovery, and the slow work of moving forward after shock.

published.fact_text: In 1990, ethnic clashes erupted in the Romanian city of Târgu Mureș between Hungarian and Romanian communities just days after commemorations of the 1848 revolutions. The violence revealed how deep historical memories and identities could resurface suddenly in a post-Communist transition.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: An episode unpacking the roots and cultural politics of a genre matches an event where identity, language, and history collide, highlighting how culture and ethnicity can both bridge and divide.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1989, the Egyptian flag was raised over Taba, a small town on the Red Sea, marking the final return of territory occupied by Israel since the 1967 Six-Day War. It was a quiet ceremony compared to the wars that preceded it, but symbolically closed a decades-long chapter with a piece of borderland and a flag.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A coastal town on the edge of Sinai and a story of borders changing hands fits a Mediterranean-themed voyage, where seas connect countries that have also known conflict and reconciliation.

published.fact_text: In 1958, a fire swept through the Monarch Underwear Company factory in Manhattan, killing 24 workers and injuring 15. The tragedy echoed earlier garment industry disasters, underscoring the hidden human cost behind everyday clothing and the importance of fire safety in cramped urban workplaces.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: A factory disaster highlights the fragile, imperfect conditions of industrial life; pairing it with an episode about imperfection and valuing the unpolished invites a more humane way of seeing work and error.

published.fact_text: On March 19, 1946, France transformed French Guiana, Guadeloupe, Martinique, and Réunion from colonies into overseas departments. This legal shift meant that these far-flung tropical lands were, on paper, integrated into France as fully as regions in Europe, even while daily life remained worlds apart.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: Caribbean and Indian Ocean territories being folded into a European state echoes the Afrobeat story of African, Caribbean, and Western influences mixing into something new and politically charged.

It was an apocalyptic command to leave nothing useful behind, invoking the burning of ancient Rome and revealing a regime ready to sacrifice its future along with itself.
